title: Functions 101 — Write Once, Use Everywhere
description: The DRY principle in action. Stop copying code. Learn to write reusable functions.
learningObjectives: I can explain what a function is and why it exists
I can write a function with parameters and a return value
I can identify when repeated code should become a function

contentSections.body: Marcus spent 3 hours building a tip calculator. Then he needed the same logic on 4 different screens. He copied and pasted the code 4 times. Then he found a bug. He had to fix it in 5 places. There's a better way.

**DRY** stands for *Don't Repeat Yourself*. It's one of the most important principles in coding.

Every time you copy-paste code, you're creating a future maintenance nightmare. When you find a bug (and you will), you have to fix it in every copy.

**Functions solve this.** A function is a named block of code that you can call from anywhere. Write it once, use it everywhere.

contentSections.answer: If the tip rate changes (or you find a bug), you have to update it in every place. Miss one and the behavior is inconsistent.
contentSections.hint: Think about what happens when you need to change the tip percentage

contentSections.body: If you're about to copy code for the second time — stop. That's your signal to write a function.
**Arrow functions** are the modern syntax. `const calculateTip = (bill, rate = 0.18) => { ... }` — same behavior, cleaner look. Both work; arrow functions are more common in modern codebases.

contentSections.question: What does a function without a `return` statement return?
contentSections.answer: `undefined`. Every function returns something. If you don't specify a return value, JavaScript returns `undefined` by default.
contentSections.hint: Think about what happens when nothing is explicitly returned

contentSections.body: A function takes inputs (parameters), does work, and returns an output. Parameters → transformation → return value.

title: Parameters, Return Values & Scope
description: Go deeper on how data flows into and out of functions. Understand why variables in functions don't leak out.
learningObjectives: I can explain the difference between parameters and arguments
I can use default parameters effectively
I can explain what scope means and why it matters

contentSections.body: Alex builds AI pipelines where the same function processes thousands of different messages. Each message is different — the function needs to handle all of them. Parameters make that possible.

contentSections.question: What happens if you call `greet('Sarah')` — without the age argument?
contentSections.answer: `age` will be `undefined`, and the output is 'Hi Sarah, you're undefined!' — no error, just unexpected output. This is why default parameters exist.
contentSections.hint: JavaScript doesn't throw an error for missing arguments — what does it fill in instead?

contentSections.body: Parameters are placeholders. Arguments are real values. Missing arguments become `undefined` — use defaults to prevent silent bugs.

contentSections.question: When is destructuring in parameters most useful?
contentSections.answer: When a function takes an object with many properties — instead of listing 5+ parameters, you destructure the object and pull out only what you need. Much cleaner than `(user.name, user.role, user.active)`.
contentSections.hint: Think about functions that take an object with many properties

contentSections.body: Default parameters prevent `undefined` surprises. Destructuring in parameters cleans up object-heavy functions.
**Scope bug:** If you forget `const` or `let`, JavaScript creates a global variable by accident — accessible everywhere. Always declare variables explicitly.

title: Reading Error Messages
description: Errors aren't failures — they're messages. Learn to decode what JavaScript is actually telling you.
learningObjectives: I can identify the type of error from an error message
I can find the file and line number where an error occurred
I can fix the 3 most common JavaScript errors

contentSections.body: Every new developer panics when they see a red error message. But here's the secret: the error message is trying to help you. It's JavaScript's way of saying 'here's exactly what went wrong, and here's where.'

A JavaScript error message has three parts:

```
TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'name')
    at getUserName (app.js:14:22)
    at main (app.js:8:5)
```

1. **Error type** (`TypeError`) — the category of error
2. **Error message** — what went wrong in plain English
3. **Stack trace** — which file, which line, which function called what

**Common error types:**
- `ReferenceError` — you used a variable that doesn't exist
- `TypeError` — you used a value in a way its type doesn't support
- `SyntaxError` — your code can't even be parsed (usually a missing bracket or semicolon)
- `RangeError` — a number is outside a valid range

contentSections.question: `ReferenceError: userName is not defined` — What probably happened?
contentSections.answer: You tried to use `userName` but either forgot to declare it (`const userName = ...`) or misspelled it (maybe it's `username` lowercase).
contentSections.hint: The error says 'is not defined' — what could cause a variable to not exist?

contentSections.body: Read errors top to bottom: type → message → first line of the stack trace. The stack trace tells you exactly where to look.

**1. Cannot read properties of undefined**
```js
const user = undefined;
console.log(user.name); // TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined
```
Fix: Check if the value exists first: `if (user) { console.log(user.name); }`

**2. is not a function**
```js
const count = 5;
count.map(x => x * 2); // TypeError: count.map is not a function
```
Fix: `map` is an array method. `count` is a number. Check your data type.

**3. Unexpected token / missing bracket**
```js
const add = (a, b => { // SyntaxError
  return a + b;
}
```
Fix: Missing `)` before `=>`. Read carefully around the line number.

contentSections.question:
What does this error mean?
`TypeError: fetch is not a function`
contentSections.answer: You're probably running this code in Node.js (not a browser). `fetch` is a browser API. In older versions of Node.js, it doesn't exist. Solution: import it with `node-fetch` or use Node 18+ which has built-in fetch.
contentSections.hint: fetch is a web browser API — where might it not exist?

contentSections.body: Most errors fall into 3 buckets: variable doesn't exist, wrong data type, or syntax mistake. 90% of bugs are one of these.

When you're stuck, Claude is your rubber duck. But how you ask matters.

**Bad prompt:**
> 'My code doesn't work, fix it'

**Good prompt:**
> 'I'm getting this error: `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'price')` on line 23 of checkout.js. Here's the relevant code: [paste 10-20 lines]. The error happens when I try to access the cart items. What's wrong?'

The magic formula: **Error message + relevant code + what you expected vs. what happened**.

Claude will identify the bug, explain why it happened, and suggest a fix. More importantly, it'll help you understand the pattern — so you recognize it next time.

**console.log is your friend.** Before asking Claude, add `console.log()` before the error line to inspect the value. Often you'll see the problem immediately: 'Oh, it's undefined because the API hasn't loaded yet.'

title: Debugging Strategies That Actually Work
description: rubber duck debugging, console.log patterns, binary search debugging. Build a systematic debugging toolkit.
learningObjectives: I can narrow down a bug using binary search debugging
I can use console.log strategically to inspect state
I can explain what rubber duck debugging is and when to use it

contentSections.body: Sarah has a function with 30 lines. Something's wrong but the error is vague. She could read every line carefully (slow). Or she could cut the problem in half: comment out lines 15-30 and test again. If it works, the bug is in lines 15-30. If it still fails, the bug is in lines 1-15. Keep halving until you find it.

**Binary search debugging** is the most efficient strategy:

1. Identify the broken behavior
2. Find the midpoint of the suspicious code
3. Add a checkpoint (console.log) there
4. Does the code reach the checkpoint with correct data? If yes → bug is after. If no → bug is before.
5. Repeat until you isolate the exact line

This works because each step cuts the search space in half. 30 lines → 15 → 8 → 4 → 2 → found.

**Most developers brute-force** — checking line by line. Binary search is 5× faster.

contentSections.question: A 100-line function has a bug. With binary search debugging, what's the maximum number of checkpoints you need to find it?
contentSections.answer: About 7. Each step halves the search space: 100 → 50 → 25 → 13 → 7 → 4 → 2 → 1. log₂(100) ≈ 7.
contentSections.hint: Each console.log cuts the problem in half. How many times can you halve 100?

contentSections.body: Don't read every line — narrow the problem space. Binary search debugging finds any bug in log₂(n) steps.

contentSections.question: Why is `console.log('user:', JSON.stringify(user, null, 2))` better than just `console.log('user:', user)` for complex objects?
contentSections.answer: When you `console.log` an object, browsers/Node show a live reference — so if the object mutates later, the logged value changes too (confusing!). JSON.stringify captures the value at that exact moment.
contentSections.hint: Objects in console.log are 'live' references — what might that cause?

contentSections.body: Label your logs. Use JSON.stringify for objects. Use console.table for arrays. Remove all debug logs before shipping.

contentSections.title: Rubber duck debugging + knowing when to ask

contentSections.body:
**Rubber duck debugging:** Explain your code out loud to an imaginary rubber duck (or Claude). The act of explaining forces you to think through each step — and often reveals the bug before you even finish explaining.

*'Okay, so first I get the user... then I get their cart... then I... wait, what if the cart is empty? I never check for that.'*

**When to ask Claude:**
- You've tried binary search and isolated the bug to 5 lines
- You've read the error message and still don't understand it
- You've spent 15+ minutes on the same bug

Don't ask immediately — the attempt to debug yourself builds pattern recognition. But don't suffer for hours either. Claude is a tool; use it strategically.
**The 15-minute rule:** Try to solve a bug yourself for 15 minutes. If you're still stuck, ask Claude. This ratio builds skills (you learn from attempts) while preventing endless frustration.

title: Thinking in Algorithms
description: An algorithm is just a recipe. Learn to break any problem into clear, ordered steps a computer can follow.
learningObjectives: I can explain what an algorithm is in plain English
I can break a real-world task into numbered steps
I can identify where a step is too vague and needs to be broken down further

contentSections.body: Every morning you make coffee. You probably don't think about it as an algorithm. But it is: boil water → measure coffee → pour into filter → wait → pour into cup → drink. That's an algorithm. Coding is the same idea — you're just writing the recipe in a language a computer understands.

An **algorithm** is a finite, ordered sequence of steps that solves a problem.

The key properties:
- **Finite** — it must end at some point
- **Ordered** — the sequence matters
- **Unambiguous** — each step must be clear enough to execute

When new developers start coding, they often try to write code immediately. The better approach:
1. Describe the solution in plain English
2. Break it into numbered steps
3. Check: is each step something a computer can do?
4. *Then* translate to code

Claude can help with step 3-4. You need to do steps 1-2 yourself — that's the thinking part.

contentSections.question: Break this into algorithm steps: 'Find the most expensive item in a shopping cart'
contentSections.answer:
1. Start with the first item as the 'current most expensive'
2. For each remaining item: compare its price to the current most expensive
3. If the new item costs more, it becomes the new 'current most expensive'
4. After checking all items, return the 'current most expensive'
contentSections.hint: Think step by step — how would you do this by hand with a list on paper?

contentSections.body: Write the algorithm in English before writing code. If you can't explain the steps in English, you can't write them in code.

**Decomposition** means breaking a complex problem into simpler sub-problems.

Example: 'Build a checkout system'

That's too big. Break it down:
- Validate the cart (has items, quantities > 0)
- Calculate total price
- Apply any discounts
- Process payment
- Update inventory
- Send confirmation email

Each of those is still complex. Break them down further:

'Apply any discounts' →
- Check if user has a promo code
- Look up promo code in database
- If valid and not expired, calculate discount amount
- Apply discount to total

Keep breaking down until each step is a single, testable function.

**Ask Claude to help you decompose.** 'I need to build X. Help me break this into the 5-8 high-level steps I need to implement.' Claude is excellent at this — it's essentially mapping your problem against thousands of similar patterns it's seen.

contentSections.question: What's the difference between an algorithm and code?
contentSections.answer: An algorithm is the idea — the logic described in any language (English, pseudocode, diagrams). Code is the implementation of that idea in a specific programming language. The algorithm comes first; the code expresses it.
contentSections.hint: Think about whether you could describe an algorithm in Spanish or on a whiteboard

contentSections.body: Decompose any big problem into smaller pieces. Each piece should be simple enough to write as a single function.

contentSections.title: Pattern recognition — solving the same problem differently

contentSections.body:
Experienced developers recognize that most problems are variations of a small number of patterns:

**Search** — find an item that matches criteria (use `.find()` or `.filter()`)
**Sort** — arrange items in order (use `.sort()`)
**Transform** — convert each item to something new (use `.map()`)
**Accumulate** — combine items into one value (use `.reduce()` or a for loop with a running total)
**Filter** — keep only items matching criteria (use `.filter()`)

When you see a new problem, ask: *is this fundamentally a search, sort, transform, accumulate, or filter?*

Most real problems are combinations of 2-3 of these patterns.

title: Arrays & Objects — Working with Data
description: Real apps have lists of things. Learn to create, read, update, and delete data in arrays and objects.
learningObjectives: I can use CRUD operations on arrays and objects
I can choose between an array and an object for a given use case
I can write code that processes a list of items

contentSections.body: Every real app has lists: a list of users, a list of messages, a list of products. If you can work with arrays, you can work with data. That's most of what apps do.

contentSections.question: What's the difference between `.push()` and `.concat()`?
contentSections.answer: `.push()` modifies the original array and returns the new length. `.concat()` returns a new array without modifying the original. In modern JS, prefer `[...arr, newItem]` (spread syntax) which also returns a new array.
contentSections.hint: Think about whether the original array changes

contentSections.body: Arrays hold ordered lists. Use `.filter()`, `.map()`, `.find()` for non-destructive operations. Use `.push()` when you want to mutate.

contentSections.question: You have a list of 1000 users. You often need to look up a specific user by ID. Should you store them as an array or as an object keyed by ID?
contentSections.answer: Object keyed by ID. With an array, finding user with ID 487 requires scanning up to 1000 items. With an object `{487: user}`, lookup is instant (O(1)). This is a fundamental data structure tradeoff.
contentSections.hint: Think about how fast you can find something in a list vs. a dictionary

contentSections.body: Use objects when you need to look things up by name or ID. Use arrays when order matters or you need to iterate over all items.

contentSections.title: Arrays of objects — the real-world pattern
